item.title: Those who day and night Are devoted to training, Learning the words of the Perfectly Enlightened One, They calm the fire of greed, Always perceiving…
item.description:
Those who day and night
Are devoted to training,
Learning the words of the Perfectly Enlightened One,
They calm the fire of greed,
Always perceiving the foulness of things.

With loving-kindness and compassion,
The foremost of persons,
They calm and still the fire of hatred.
And the fire of delusion
They extinguish with wisdom,
Wisdom that leads to penetration of the truth.

The vigilant, the wise,
Day and night they calm such fires,
Without weariness.
They calm them completely, leaving no trace.

The noble ones who have seen,
The wise who know,
Are the truly wise, seeing with right knowledge.
Having fully known the destruction of birth,
They do not go to birth again and again.

From: Itivuttaka 93
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: 'Venerable Nun, what is the counterpart of pleasant feeling?' — 'Friend Visākha, painful feeling is the counterpart of pleasant feeling.' — 'Venerable Nun, what is…
item.description:
'Venerable Nun, what is the counterpart of pleasant feeling?' — 'Friend Visākha, painful feeling is the counterpart of pleasant feeling.' — 'Venerable Nun, what is the counterpart of painful feeling?' — 'Friend Visākha, pleasant feeling is the counterpart of painful feeling.' — 'Venerable Nun, what is the counterpart of neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling?' — 'Friend Visākha, ignorance is the counterpart of neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling.' — 'Venerable Nun, what is the counterpart of ignorance?' — 'Friend Visākha, knowledge is the counterpart of ignorance.' — 'Venerable Nun, what is the counterpart of knowledge?' — 'Friend Visākha, liberation is the counterpart of knowledge.' — 'Venerable Nun, what is the counterpart of liberation?' — 'Friend Visākha, Nibbāna is the counterpart of liberation.' — 'Venerable Nun, what is the counterpart of Nibbāna?' — 'Friend Visākha, your question goes too far, beyond the limit of an answer. Friend Visākha, the holy life is grounded in Nibbāna, culminates in Nibbāna, and ends in Nibbāna.'

From: Majjhima Nikāya 44 – Cūḷavedallasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Then Māra, having changed into the form of a brahmin with a large topknot, wearing an antelope skin, old, bent like a roof beam, breathing…
item.description:
Then Māra, having changed into the form of a brahmin with a large topknot, wearing an antelope skin, old, bent like a roof beam, breathing heavily, holding a staff of udumbara wood, went to those bhikkhus and said: 'Venerable sirs, you have gone forth too young, in your prime, with black hair, in the bloom of youth, in the spring of life, not yet enjoying sensual pleasures. Enjoy human sensual pleasures, venerable sirs, do not neglect the present to run after what is affected by time.'

- Brahmin, we do not neglect the present to run after what is affected by time. And, brahmin, we neglect what is affected by time to run after the present. Brahmin, the Blessed One has said that sensual pleasures are affected by time, full of suffering, full of despair; the danger there is even greater. But this Dhamma is present, not affected by time, inviting inspection, leading onward, and to be experienced by the wise for themselves.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 4.21
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: These three ascetic perceptions, bhikkhus, when developed and cultivated, fulfill seven things. What seven? He is constantly one who acts continuously, practicing continuously in the…
item.description:
These three ascetic perceptions, bhikkhus, when developed and cultivated, fulfill seven things. What seven? He is constantly one who acts continuously, practicing continuously in the rules of conduct; without craving; without hatred; without over-conceit, delighting in training; regarding the basic necessities of life, he thinks: 'This is the purpose of living energetically.' Bhikkhus, these three ascetic perceptions, when developed and cultivated, fulfill these seven things.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 10.101
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Rāhula, develop the development like air. Rāhula, by developing like air, agreeable and disagreeable contacts that arise do not take hold of the mind, do…
item.description:
Rāhula, develop the development like air. Rāhula, by developing like air, agreeable and disagreeable contacts that arise do not take hold of the mind, do not remain. Rāhula, just as air blows on pure things, impure things, excrement, urine, spittle, pus, and blood, yet air is not troubled, not agitated, not disgusted — so too, Rāhula, develop like air. Rāhula, by developing like air, agreeable and disagreeable contacts that arise do not take hold of the mind, do not remain.

From: Majjhima Nikāya 62 – Mahārāhulovādasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Ānanda, before my enlightenment, while I was still a bodhisatta, not yet fully enlightened, I thought: 'Good is renunciation! Good is dwelling in seclusion!' But…
item.description:
Ānanda, before my enlightenment, while I was still a bodhisatta, not yet fully enlightened, I thought: 'Good is renunciation! Good is dwelling in seclusion!' But my mind did not become inspired in that renunciation, did not become serene, did not become established, did not become liberated, even though I saw: 'This is peaceful.' Then, Ānanda, I thought: 'For what reason, for what condition, does my mind not become inspired in that renunciation, not serene, not established, not liberated, even though I see: "This is peaceful"?' Then, Ānanda, I thought: 'Because I do not see the danger in sensual pleasures, because I have not cultivated that danger, and the benefit of renunciation has not been attained, I have not tasted that benefit. Therefore my mind does not become inspired in that renunciation, not serene, not established, not liberated.' Then, Ānanda, I thought: 'If, after seeing the danger in sensual pleasures, I cultivate that danger, and after attaining the benefit in renunciation, I taste that benefit,' then this is possible: my mind may become inspired in renunciation, serene, established, liberated, because I see: 'This is peaceful.'

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 9.41
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Those bhikkhus, having completed the rains retreat, after three months, started wandering. A devata dwelling in that forest, not seeing those bhikkhus, lamented and grieved,…
item.description:
Those bhikkhus, having completed the rains retreat, after three months, started wandering. A devata dwelling in that forest, not seeing those bhikkhus, lamented and grieved, and on that occasion spoke this verse:

Today my mind
Feels no joy,
Seeing many seats
Empty, without people.
Those learned ones,
Who taught the Dhamma so beautifully,
Disciples of Gotama,
Where are they now?"

Hearing this, another devata spoke these verses to that devata:

They have gone to Magadha,
They have gone to Kosala,
And some of them
Have gone to the Vajji country.
Like deer freed from a trap,
They run about in all directions.
Bhikkhus have no homes,
Thus they live liberated.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 9.4
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Friends, here there is a duty a bhikkhu has to do. He thinks: 'There is work I must do. If I do the work, my…
item.description:
Friends, here there is a duty a bhikkhu has to do. He thinks: 'There is work I must do. If I do the work, my body will become tired. So I shall lie down.' He lies down, without striving to attain the unattained, to achieve the unachieved, to realize the unrealized. That is the first way of being slothful.

From: Dīgha Nikāya 33 – Saṅgītisutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: And, bhikkhus, what is a person with inverted wisdom? Here, bhikkhus, a person goes to the monastery and often hears the Dhamma from the bhikkhus.…
item.description:
And, bhikkhus, what is a person with inverted wisdom? Here, bhikkhus, a person goes to the monastery and often hears the Dhamma from the bhikkhus. The bhikkhus teach him the Dhamma, beautiful in the beginning, beautiful in the middle, beautiful in the end, with meaning and phrasing, revealing the completely perfected and purified holy life. While sitting there, he does not attend to the beginning of that discourse, does not attend to the middle, does not attend to the end. Likewise, when he rises from that seat, he does not attend to the beginning, does not attend to the middle, does not attend to the end. Just as, bhikkhus, an overturned pot pours out the water contained in it and does not retain it, so too, bhikkhus, here a person goes to the monastery... does not attend to the end. Likewise, when he rises from that seat, he does not attend to the beginning... does not attend to the end. This, bhikkhus, is called a person with inverted wisdom.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 3.30
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: 'What leads the world along? What torments oneself? Is there one thing To which everything is subject?' 'Craving leads the world along, Craving torments oneself,…
item.description:
'What leads the world along?
What torments oneself?
Is there one thing
To which everything is subject?'

'Craving leads the world along,
Craving torments oneself,
Craving is that one thing
To which everything is subject.'

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 1.63
📖 Read this sutta
